verdict_reasoning: HSPD1 (heat shock protein family D member 1, also known as Hsp60) is canonically a mitochondrial-matrix chaperone resident in the inner mitochondrial compartment. However, it has documented surface display under cell-state conditions: (1) stress-induced externalization — ER stress, heat shock, and oxidative stress trigger translocation of HSPD1 to the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane and the extracellular space in multiple cell types; (2) cancer-cell ecto-display — tumor cells display HSPD1 on the outer face as an ecto-chaperone (cancer ecto-Hsp60), facilitating tumor-stromal cross-talk and immune evasion; (3) cell-death pathways — apoptotic and necroptotic cells externalizing HSPD1 to the surface as a damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP). These non-baseline surface pools qualify as contextual surface accessibility. HSPD1 is also a validated clinical CAR-T target in multiple myeloma and solid tumors, with programs targeting the surface-displayed pool. The protein's own mechanism for ecto-display appears stress-mediated PM translocation or specialized exocytosis; the baseline compartment is mitochondrial-internal, but the documented stress-induced and cancer-cell-restricted surface pools are stable enough for therapeutic engagement.
